title: Appium vs Selenium
overview: Both use the WebDriver protocol, but Appium extends it for mobile: additional commands for gestures, app management, device interaction. Appium acts as a 'mobile Selenium' with vendor-specific drivers.
whyItMatters: Understanding the differences ensures you don't try to apply web-centric automation paradigms directly to mobile apps, and you leverage mobile-specific commands correctly.
explanation: Selenium is built for web browsers, executing commands to interact with the DOM via ChromeDriver/GeckoDriver. Appium is built for mobile devices, translating WebDriver commands into UIAutomator2 (Android) or XCUITest (iOS) commands. Appium extends the protocol for mobile features: installing apps, locking the screen, touch actions, and rotating the device. While Selenium uses CSS/XPath natively, Appium relies on Accessibility IDs and platform-specific locators.
keyPoints: Appium is built on top of the WebDriver protocol.
Selenium targets browsers; Appium targets mobile (native, hybrid, and mobile web).
Appium provides mobile-specific APIs (e.g. TouchActions, Context switching).
realWorldExample: If your company has a web portal and a native mobile app, you would use Selenium for the portal and Appium for the app, but both could share the same reporting framework (TestNG/ExtentReports) because of the shared WebDriver foundation.
commonMistakes: Using Selenium locators (CSS) for native mobile app elements.
Trying to use driver.get(url) to launch a native app (you must use app capabilities instead).
bestPractices: Design cross-platform page objects where possible, but maintain separate locators for iOS and Android.
Use Appium-specific drivers (`AndroidDriver`, `IOSDriver`) instead of plain `WebDriver` to access mobile methods.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the primary difference between Appium and Selenium?
interviewQuestions.a: Selenium is strictly for web browser automation, while Appium extends the WebDriver protocol to automate mobile applications (native, hybrid) and offers mobile-specific actions.

revisionSummary: Appium is 'Selenium for Mobile'. It uses the WebDriver protocol but extends it with commands for touch gestures, device management, and app context switching, using platform-specific drivers like UiAutomator2 and XCUITest.
